# Research Notes: Persuasion Techniques

## Step
- Step 3 of Plan (20260401-concurrent-01)

## Sources Used
- src-005 (Purdue OWL — Rhetorical Strategies: Aristotelian Appeals) — ACQ-003, passed

## Key Findings

### Ethos (Credibility)
Persuasive appeal based on character and credibility of the speaker/writer. Established through: demonstrating expertise, establishing common ground, showing fairness (acknowledging counterarguments), maintaining consistency. Aristotle distinguished "prior ethos" (existing reputation) from "invented ethos" (credibility established within the speech/text). In writing, invented ethos is particularly important because readers often have no prior knowledge of the writer (src-005).

### Pathos (Emotional Appeal)
Appeal to the audience's emotions. Aristotle: "The orator persuades by means of his hearers, when they are roused to emotion by his speech" (Rhetoric I.2). Effective use includes: vivid language and imagery, narrative and anecdote, tone and word choice, appeals to values. Pathos is not manipulation — legitimate emotional appeals illuminate significance of an issue; fallacious appeals substitute emotion for evidence (src-005).

### Logos (Logical Appeal)
Appeal based on logic and reasoning. Encompasses argument structure and evidence. Includes: deductive reasoning (syllogisms), inductive reasoning (specific to general), evidence (facts, statistics, expert testimony), logical structure. Aristotle's enthymeme (rhetorical syllogism with unstated premise) is "the body of persuasion" — engages audience by requiring them to supply the missing premise (src-005).

### Kairos (Timeliness)
Not one of the original three appeals, but increasingly recognized as a fourth dimension. Addresses the question of WHEN to make an argument. Considers: current context and relevance, audience readiness, appropriateness of medium and format (src-005).

### Note on Downgraded Source
src-006 (Britannica — Persuasion) was downgraded in verification due to scope extending into general psychology rather than writing-specific persuasion. However, it provides useful supplementary context on modern persuasion theory (ELM model by Petty and Cacioppo, Cialdini's Principles of Influence). These are noted here for completeness but the primary knowledge grounding is from src-005.
